data.title: ‘Easier targets’: is Japan safe for foreigners if disaster strikes?
data.description:
A century after thousands of foreigners were massacred in Japan following a devastating earthquake, human rights advocates worry about rising vigilantism should disaster strike again.
It follows calls from the mayor of at least one Japanese city for citizens to form “vigilante groups” in response to reports of rising crime by foreign nationals.
“This is symptomatic of Japan’s changing demographics and the rise of xenophobia,” Teppei Kasai of Human Rights Watch in Tokyo told This Week in Asia, pointing to the unprecedented number of foreign workers and tourists the country had seen in recent years.

A government panel assessing the possible impact of a major Tokyo earthquake warned in December of the potential for communications breakdown and unrest in the aftermath of a natural disaster, citing the Great Kanto Earthquake of 1923 as a historical precedent.
Historians estimate that 6,000 Koreans, Chinese and other foreign nationals were killed in Japan that year after baseless rumours accused them of poisoning water supplies and looting following the earthquake. Far-right groups deny the massacre ever happened.
Foreign residents and relief workers were similarly accused of committing crimes in the aftermath of the earthquake and tsunami that struck Japan in 2011. Unfounded claims of “foreign robbers” also spread on social media after a quake hit Ishikawa prefecture in January 2024.


‘Easier targets’
History showed that “easily identifiable targets tend to be singled out” during times of crisis, said Lee Yoomi, chair of a non-profit organisation in Kobe that provides language and translation services.
“If there are rumours, misunderstandings or negative perceptions about foreigners, they become easier targets,” she said, adding that there was an “increasingly noticeable xenophobic atmosphere” in Japanese society nowadays.
Lee, who has Korean ancestry, said she had faced subtle forms of discrimination in the past when applying for jobs or looking for housing – and though this had not “overtly” worsened, the rise of “Japanese first” rhetoric was a concern.
“Recently, family members and foreign friends around me often express deep anxiety about the future,” she told This Week in Asia. “I myself feel uneasy, too.”

She said her organisation, Multilingual Centre Facil, had received several complaints of foreign residents being harassed and verbally abused.
Anti-immigrant sentiment also flared ahead of last July’s Upper House election, when conspiracy theories about immigrants receiving lavish government benefits circulated widely online.
The far-right Sanseito party, known for its anti-immigration stance and “Japanese first” rhetoric, performed strongly in that poll.
Japan’s foreign resident population hit a record last year, exceeding 3.95 million or more than 3 per cent of the total, while nearly 40 million overseas tourists visited the country.
Vigilante groups
In places like Gunma prefecture, west of Tokyo, cultural tensions have collided with public unease about foreigners committing crimes after police reported a 48 per cent jump in home burglaries in one year and the arrests of Vietnamese nationals for theft made headlines.
Takasaki, the prefectural capital, responded by offering subsidies for daytime “vigilante groups” made up of residents and community associations, with local firefighters and private security firms to patrol at night.
“Protecting citizens’ lives from violent crime requires not only public assistance but also self-help and mutual assistance,” Mayor Kenji Tomioka said in a September statement.
“I urge you to promote the formation of vigilante groups and patrol activities.”
Supporters online cheered the initiative, calling for nationwide patrols, tighter border controls and even roadside spot checks for foreigners. One comment on the Yahoo News website accused the government of pushing “multicultural coexistence without looking at the reality”. Others demanded “random inspections of personal belongings” and drug testing for non-Japanese residents.
Gunma prefecture has long attracted Brazilians to work in its factories and now has a growing Vietnamese community. Around 4 per cent of the population is foreign born.

Kasai of Human Rights Watch sees in these sentiments a dangerous normalisation of prejudice.
“The focus should be on how to address crime rather than how to target a specific group of people based on their ethnicity or nationality,” he said.
Citing the rising numbers of migrant workers and tourists in Japan, Kasai said the country was “experiencing something it has never had to deal with directly before”.
Japan has no independent human rights commission and no law explicitly prohibiting racial or ethnic discrimination.
Kasai urged Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s administration to establish an independent human rights body, as recommended by the United Nations, and to pass anti-discrimination legislation.
Tokyo needed “policies that are centred on human rights”, he said, not those that “just stoke xenophobic sentiment”.
Since taking office in October, Takichi has pushed tighter regulations on foreign nationals in Japan, citing public order and national security.

data.title: American-Chinese teen fencer Liu ready to overcome tough start in ‘year of growth’
data.description:
After a tough start to her 2026 season, American-Chinese teenager Jaelyn Liu believes this is the year to learn what it takes to become a force in the fencing world.
The 16-year-old burst onto the stage in Hong Kong a year ago, becoming the youngest winner of an individual foil World Cup event in the sport’s history.
Defending her title last weekend, the best the United States team member could manage was 20th, to go along with her 35th places in previous legs in Mallorca and Busan.
It represents something of a come down for the Texas teen, whose father is from Fujian and her mother from Sichuan.
Last year, she jumped from 93rd to 13th in the world, added the Junior and Cadet Fencing World Championship titles to her growing collection, and claimed team gold alongside some of her more decorated teammates, including world No 1 Lee Kiefer and Lauren Scruggs.

“After all that success, I’ve been in the clouds, I am not really solid in my emotions or any way,” she told the Post. “I believe this year is a time to grow.
“I had some hiccups in my first three senior competitions. I didn’t perform as well as I hoped. Everyone has these lows, especially psychological barriers, which I feel like I’m facing right now, and I know I’ll overcome them.”
Ralf Bissdorf, the US women’s foil head coach, is not that concerned by the dip in form, believing his pupil just needs “a little bit of consistency”, and urged her not to force things.
“She’s dominating the cadets and juniors worldwide, and in the seniors, there will be ups and downs, but we are ready,” he said. “She’s not at the end of her fencing, personal and tactical development; she’s on track.
“Progress is not always linear, and we will see those dips. Sometimes it’s good if it gets a little bit harder in between … it builds character, resilience and hunger to do better.”
The 54-year-old German, who won a silver medal at the 2000 Sydney Olympics and took charge of the US team after the Tokyo Games in 2022, said Liu was definitely an important part of his plans in the build-up to Los Angeles 2028.
“It’s a privilege for a national coach to have somebody that young [but] already that far up in the world rankings and that competitive,” Bissdorf said. “She’s definitely going to be an asset for the future, and we’re looking forward to seeing what she’s going to be capable of doing.”
There is no need to rush things either. Liu still has a year competing as a cadet and three more in juniors, although also fencing on the senior tour at the same time is “kind of tiring”.
“[But] it builds a lot of grit and endurance, we do fencing for the love of the sport, it’s not our job,” she said. “So the only way you can enjoy the sport is to have passion in it, not just by winning.
“So, it’s going to be a long journey, but I hope to get better as a senior, to get my skill level on par.”
Hongkongers will get the opportunity to see how that is coming along in July, when the World Fencing Championships come to the city, and for Liu returning to “one of my favourite places” is an added bonus.
“I never went to Hong Kong [until last year] and being able to win was just so unforgettable,” Liu said. “I still love Hong Kong, and I love the people here because I feel related to them all.”

data.title: Mexican president tells Trump that US intervention against drug cartels is ‘unnecessary’
data.description:
Mexico’s President Claudia Sheinbaum said she had “a very good conversation” with US President Donald Trump on Monday and that their two governments will continue working together on security issues without the need for US intervention against drug cartels.
The roughly 15-minute call came after Sheinbaum said on Friday she had requested dialogue with the Trump administration at the end of a week in which he had said he was ready to confront drug cartels on the ground and repeated the accusation that cartels were running Mexico.
Trump has repeatedly offered to send the US military after the cartels and Sheinbaum has always declined, but after the US removal of Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro, Trump’s comments about Mexico, Cuba and Greenland carried new weight.
“He (Trump) asked me my opinion about what they had done in Venezuela and I told him very clearly that our constitution is very clear, that we do not agree with interventions and that was it,” Sheinbaum said.
Trump “still insisted that if we ask for it, they could help” with military forces, which Sheinbaum said she again rejected. “We told him, so far it’s going very well, it’s not necessary, and furthermore there is Mexico’s sovereignty and territorial integrity and he understood.”
In an interview with Fox News broadcast on Thursday, Trump said: “We’ve knocked out 97 per cent of the drugs coming in by water and we are going to start now hitting land, with regard to the cartels. The cartels are running Mexico. It’s very sad to watch.”


Sheinbaum said on Monday the two leaders agreed to continue working together.
Mexico’s Foreign Affairs Secretary Juan Ramon de la Fuente spoke on Sunday to his US counterpart, Secretary of State Marco Rubio. Rubio asked for “tangible results” and more cooperation to dismantle the cartels, according to a statement from the US State Department.
Sheinbaum said Mexico shared those results, including a significant drop in murders, falling US fentanyl seizures and fentanyl overdose deaths.
Experts still see US intervention in Mexico as unlikely because Mexico is doing what the US asks and is a critical economic partner, but expect Trump to continue using such rhetoric to maintain pressure on Mexico to do more.
Sheinbaum said the two leaders did not speak about Cuba, which Trump threatened on Sunday. Mexico is an important ally of the island nation, including selling it oil that it will need even more desperately now that the Trump administration says it will not allow any more oil shipments from Venezuela to Cuba.

data.title: China’s weight-loss drug war: prices slashed up to 80% as obesity crisis worsens
data.description:
Foreign and mainland Chinese drug makers are fighting for a multibillion-dollar slice of the domestic weight-loss market by slashing prices by as much as 80 per cent, as China faces a worsening obesity crisis.
Competition in the sector, dominated by global pharmaceutical giants Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly, intensified after both secured obesity-drug approvals in China in 2024. The landscape is set to shift further when the patent on Novo Nordisk’s semaglutide expires in March in China, opening the door for a wave of Chinese generics ready to challenge their dominance.
The price war began in late December after Novo Nordisk cut Wegovy prices by half in southwestern Chinese provinces including Yunnan and Sichuan, according to Chinese media.
“Defending market share takes precedence over margins,” said Cui Cui, head of healthcare research for Asia at US investment bank Jefferies. “Weight-loss drugs carry high gross margins, enabling manufacturers to absorb price reductions.”
Wegovy, a once-weekly injection using semaglutide, saw monthly treatment costs for its highest doses fall from around 1,900 yuan (US$272) at launch to under 1,000 yuan. Online platforms such as Taobao and JD.com also slashed prices. Taobao is a unit of Post owner Alibaba Group Holding’s Tmall unit.
A one-month supply of Eli Lilly’s Mounjaro 2.4-millilitre dose sells for about 500 yuan on JD.com, lower than the discounted prices seen on Taobao, representing about an 80 per cent drop from its initial launch price.
“Multinational corporations cut prices faster and deeper than our expectation to compete in this market,” said Zhang Jialin, Nomura’s head of China healthcare research. “Local peers should follow.”

Yang Huang, head of China healthcare research at JPMorgan Chase, said smaller GLP-1 players in China might not withstand such competition and could exit the market sooner than expected.
The number of overweight or obese adults in China is expected to climb in the coming decades. A study published in The Lancet last year showed that more than 400 million adults met this threshold in 2021, and the total could reach about 630 million by 2050. Obese is defined as having a body mass index – weight divided by the square of height – of over 30. Treatment would cost 418 billion yuan, the study revealed.
Chinese drug makers are capitalising on the looming patent cliff and growing demand to churn out GLP-1 therapies. More than 60 GLP-1 drug candidates were undergoing late-stage clinical trials in China that could rival semaglutide and Mounjaro, according to a report by Boston-based L.E.K. Consulting.
Vying for a larger share of the market where GLP‑1 penetration remained low, Chinese novel drug developer Innovent Biologics slashed the price of its drug mazdutide by about 40 per cent in early January, according to Jefferies. Mazdutide, the first home-grown obesity drug that was approved in June last year, was priced 2,920 yuan per month for the highest dosage at launch.
“Chinese firms will have to price their drugs even lower, as they do not have the halo effect of large global studies to corroborate their efficacy,” said Tony Ren, head of Asia healthcare research at Macquarie Capital. “Obesity in China could ultimately become a consumer-focused, recreational and aesthetics play.”
Macquarie Capital estimated mazdutide could reach peak sales of 8.6 billion yuan in 2031, with 80 per cent of revenue coming from obesity treatment and the remainder from diabetes drugs.
Other contenders in late-stage clinical trials include CSPC Pharmaceutical Group’s once-weekly GLP-1 Fc fusion protein TG103, which is preparing for regulatory submission, and Jiangsu Hengrui Pharmaceuticals’ HRS9531, which completed Phase III trials and submitted a new drug application last year.
Huadong Medicine’s semaglutide injection and oral small molecule GLP-1 agonist for weight management indication have also completed Phase III trials.
To ease the blow from patent expiries, Novo Nordisk in March last year paid US$200 million to China’s United Laboratories for global rights, excluding Greater China, to a drug developed for obesity, type 2 diabetes and other metabolic diseases.

data.title: Amid Back to the Past’s success, Hong Kong cinema should look to the future
data.description:
Hong Kong’s film industry has made a promising start to the new year with science fiction blockbuster Back to the Past hitting box office records. But with overall takings dropping in recent years and more cinemas having shut down – the latest being Emperor Cinemas in Ma On Shan – the way forward for the creative industry remains challenging. Bagging no less than HK$45.4 million (US$5.8 million), the time-travel story based on a popular television drama series in 2001 has overtaken A Guilty Conscience and The Last Dance to become the highest-grossing film in its opening week.
The film, about a special agent stranded by a time machine in the Qin dynasty, already set a record for the first-day performance of a local or mainland Chinese film by grossing HK$10.91 million when it debuted on December 31, coming second to the all-time record of HK$20.9 million set by Avengers: Endgame on its opening day in 2019. Whether its success bodes well for the industry in the coming years remains to be seen. But it takes more than a blockbuster to sustain healthy development.
According to data compiled by Hong Kong Box Office Limited, local earnings sank 15.8 per cent from 2024 to HK$1.13 billion in 2025, a 14-year new low. Last year’s best-performing locally produced film, Another World, only made HK$15.1 million, a fraction of the highest grossing of HK$97.6 million by another animation, the Japanese production Demon Slayer: Kimetsu no Yaiba Infinity Castle.
The policy address in 2023 committed an injection of HK$4.3 billion to the Film Development Fund and the CreateSmart Initiative to provide incentives for attracting private sector capital and expanding new markets. Hopefully, it can foster better development.
The heyday of the Hollywood of the East flooding local and overseas markets with comedies, martial arts and triad films is over. But it is too early to mourn the death of the industry when some productions continue to capture the hearts of audiences near and far.
The success of individual films featuring local elements or social issues underlines the diversified taste of the audience. Stories that resonate still have a strong appeal, although they have sadly become rarer as the industry struggles to reinvent itself and adapt to the fast-changing world of entertainment.
The wave of cinema shutdowns amid growing competition from global streaming platforms speaks volumes about the challenges ahead. Even though the experience of enjoying a good film in a cinema hall may never be replaced by the convenience of clicking on television screens and tablets, original and captivating stories and acting are needed to reward a trip to the cinema. The city must compete on quality instead of quantity.

data.title: Year of the Horse 2026 predictions for Roosters, from love and health to their lucky colour
data.description:
From auspicious horse-themed phrases and couplets to whether your luck is in, check out our Year of the Horse 2026 series to discover all you need to know about the coming Lunar New Year.
What does the Year of the Horse 2026 have in store for those born in the Year of the Rooster (1933, 1945, 1957, 1969, 1981, 1993, 2005, 2017)? Renowned fortune-teller Tong Pik-ha offers her comprehensive predictions for the year.
Lucky numbers: 5, 10; lucky colour: dark brown; lucky element: tiger’s eye
The Year of the Horse will be a strong one for Roosters, with an abundance of lucky stars set to see things go from good to great.
There will be a significant boost in wealth and career luck, allowing for more efficient work – though it is crucial to seize opportunities and build a solid foundation for your career. Romance is also on the cards.

Wealth and career
This year will bring abundant good fortune under the influence of not one, but two lucky stars. These will positively affect the wealth of those born in the Year of the Rooster, boosting their luck when it comes to windfalls.
Those who are self-employed, and business owners in female-related industries such as jewellery, beauty, cosmetics, baby products or women’s clothing, will prosper.
Employees will also benefit from salary increases and bonuses, and will gain the appreciation of benefactors with female energy, such as female superiors. Those considering a job change should act, as they are likely to find a suitable position with good pay.
The presence of a lucky star in the charts means there will be potential profits from investments. This is a good time to explore diversified investment portfolios, including stocks, bonds and funds, and seize opportunities to earn additional income outside work.
However, there is a bad star that suggests a tendency to get entangled with others over money matters; caution should be taken when entering contracts and striking deals.

Relationships
The Year of the Horse is one with a strong romance star, bringing a surge in romantic luck for those born in the Year of the Rooster. Single women may meet excellent and compatible partners, and taking part in social gatherings should increase this likelihood.
Single men can also take advantage of this auspicious year to meet their ideal partner and may even have the chance to rekindle a former flame and develop a long-term relationship.
Couples planning to have a child should consult a traditional Chinese medicine practitioner to prepare for pregnancy.
Roosters in stable relationships may consider getting married in the Year of the Horse. The strong romance star symbolises fairy-tale endings, making the coming year a suitable one to tie the knot.

Health
Those born in the Year of the Rooster look set to enjoy luck when it comes to health in the Year of the Horse. Previous illnesses will respond to treatment; however, health problems will be common due to busy work schedules and insufficient rest, so maintaining a healthy lifestyle will be crucial.
Roosters should establish a reasonable sleep schedule to ensure sufficient rest each night. They should also maintain a balanced diet, increasing their intake of fruits and vegetables while eating fewer high-fat and high-sugar foods and avoiding excessive alcohol consumption and smoking.
Regular exercise, such as walking, swimming or gym workouts, can enhance cardiovascular function and boost immunity. Body checks to detect any potential health problems are suggested.
Are your loved ones in luck this year? See our predictions for all the zodiac signs in the Year of the Horse.

data.title: Trump says countries that trade with Iran will face 25 per cent tariffs from US
data.description:
US President Donald Trump on Monday announced sweeping new tariffs targeting nations maintaining commercial ties with Iran.
Effective immediately, the US will impose a 25 per cent tariff on all goods and services imported from any country that “does business” with Iran, Trump said in a social media post.
The president characterised the order as “final and conclusive”.
While order is global, it is expected to hit major economies, specifically China and India, the hardest, given their significant energy and commercial ties with Tehran.
More to follow...

data.title: Expanded Hong Kong-mainland rail links can be engine of growth
data.description:
When it comes to travelling by rail, connectivity is as important as speed. The latest expansion of direct high-speed rail access to 16 more mainland Chinese destinations is set to boost both the popularity of railway travel and cross-border integration and tourism development. The city should seize the opportunity and capitalise on the full economic potential of the new rail services.
The additional stops, which primarily cover the Yangtze River Delta region and Guangdong province, increase the number of direct-access destinations to 110. These include Hefei in Anhui and Miluo East in Hunan. Travellers can also reach as far as Nanjing and Wuxi in Jiangsu province without transfers. In response to strong demand, the sleeper train service to Shanghai’s Hongqiao station will also be increased from four pairs a week to running daily.
It will be up to individual travellers to choose whether to travel by air or rail. Currently, a direct train journey to Wuxi takes eight hours at the cost of HK$1,042 (US$134), while a flight cuts the trip to under three hours for around HK$800. Travel agencies have welcomed the expansion and are expected to study the feasibility of new offers in light of costs and benefits.
The expanded network offers travellers more options and further unlocks the economic potential of the high-speed rail service amid the city’s intensifying economic and social integration with the mainland. The increase in longer-distance destinations can benefit local tourism and related industries as travellers from those places tend to stay longer and spend more.
Passengers using the cross-border high-speed rail made a record 30 million-plus trips last year, a 17 per cent rise from 2024. The West Kowloon rail terminus reached a new single-day record of 140,000 passenger trips shortly before the new year. Cumulative patronage since the service launched in September 2018 has surpassed 100 million journeys, proving the city’s HK$84.4 billion railway project linking Hong Kong to the national network to be a worthy venture. Hopefully the high-speed railway will act as an engine of growth and further fast-track cross-border integration and economic development.

data.title: The US has played its hand in Latin America. Will China’s firms there cash out?
data.description:
With domestic profits narrowing and production capacity expanding, China’s firms are continuing to widen their overseas footprints in search of new, more lucrative markets. In this series, we examine China Inc.’s next phase of “going global” and the complex, challenging international environment its companies have chosen to enter.
In the days since Washington’s ousting and abduction of former Venezuelan president Nicolas Maduro – an event that sent shock waves through global markets – some Chinese business executives say they are already being inundated with pitches from consultancies offering their services in risk management.
“Recently, there has been a surge in promotional materials from various advisory firms touting overseas risk-mitigation strategies,” said one executive at a consumer goods manufacturer based in China’s eastern Zhejiang province.
He declined to be named due to the sensitivity of the topic, but said overseas expansion has been on the company’s radar for several years, making his company a prime target for the consultants’ entreaties.
The episode highlighted the rising need for risk management among Chinese firms looking to expand abroad, he said, but did little to slow what many see as an irreversible push outward, driven by shrinking domestic opportunities and the growing benefits of localisation.
Chinese entrepreneurs in Latin America, jolted by the political turmoil in Venezuela, said they have become more attuned to geopolitical risks, but also recognise the region is not a monolithic bloc. Each country, they said, must be approached with a tailored strategy.
Local communities in Venezuela are watching the turmoil closely, but as it has not yet amounted to a substantive transfer of power or regime collapse, short-term disruptions have been limited, said businessman He Zuo. Currently in Chile, he spent years in Venezuela, and many of his family members are still there.
“People are more concerned about whether society is in order, rather than political slogans,” he added, noting that daily business and logistics have continued normally.
“Political trajectories are diverging rather than moving in lockstep in Latin America,” he said, adding that Chile remains relatively stable and is “less directly exposed to Sino-US rivalry”.




He described Chile as a society that is “more rational, institutionally mature and relatively affluent”, adding that even with a shift to a right-leaning government, policy changes have been moderate and political turnover has had limited impact on the business environment.
Zhao Zhijiang, a researcher with Beijing-based think tank Anbound, stressed that Latin America is far from a homogeneous market, adding that Chinese companies can no longer assess the region through a single “Latin America logic” and must instead adopt a more “granular, country- and sector-specific” system of risk analysis.
The core question, he said, is not whether Chinese firms should continue going abroad, but how to avoid being passively exposed to external shocks. While the trend of Chinese companies expanding into Latin America will not reverse, he said, “investment decisions are set to become more cautious, with growing reliance on commercial intelligence and geopolitical risk assessment.”
China’s footprint in Central and South America has grown markedly in recent years, driven by investments in energy, ports and strategic minerals. Entrepreneurs are attracted to the region’s abundant resources, large and emerging consumer markets, and sectors in early development with rich growth potential.
According to a PwC report published in November, China’s direct investment flows into Latin America reached US$15.56 billion in 2024. This was an increase of 15.4 per cent year on year, with the region ranking as China’s second-largest overseas recipient of capital.
By the end of 2024, the professional services firm said, China’s accumulated FDI stock in Latin America exceeded US$600 billion, accounting for about 20 per cent of the country's total outbound investments.
But approaches to doing business vary within the region. Countries such as Mexico, Brazil, Chile and Peru differ fundamentally from Venezuela in terms of institutional stability, foreign investment regimes and external relations, with their own promises and complications.
Chinese entrepreneurs in Mexico told the Post the turmoil in Venezuela has not affected their local operations, with most describing sentiment on the ground as calm but watchful. Personnel at consumer goods and manufacturing companies said activities are continuing as normal.


“Over the longer term, there will definitely be implications – it will depend on how the situation unfolds,” said Javier Liu, a Chinese national with an auto parts and road materials business in Mexico, adding that his enterprise has not yet felt any direct impact.
Mexico, with its relatively stable political environment, predictable investment rules and proximity to the US market, functions as a convenient manufacturing and logistics hub for Chinese firms.
The country has become China’s second-largest Latin American trading partner, with US$109 billion in activity recorded in 2024. Among the most popular sectors are manufacturing, auto parts and consumer goods.
Brazil, another major destination for Chinese firms, has been largely insulated from developments in Venezuela. A member of the Brics bloc of emerging economies, it has maintained a broadly balanced approach towards China across administrations, with ties further strengthened under President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva.
Defined by scale and strategic autonomy rather than reliance on any single power, Brazil has maintained resilient economic ties with China – especially in trade, agriculture and manufacturing – across political cycles. Its large domestic market and relatively institutionalised policymaking give Chinese firms room to localise and plan for the long term, even as US influence remains strong in finance and commodities.
Eric Chen, a Hangzhou-based entrepreneur who owns a lighting business with a local warehouse and team in Brazil, said the Venezuela crisis has not tarnished his expectations for the South American market.
“It’s far away, for one, and Brazil’s ties with the US are quite solid,” he said, noting that many big deals in critical sectors are backed by US capital.
“Brazil is vast, with a large population, and it is the core market in South America. For my company, going deep into one market is more than enough.”
[They] want to invest more aggressively into the market … they don’t care about the geopolitics
Jack Lee, Grand Central Advisory
China, Brazil’s largest trading partner, more than doubled its foreign direct investment in the country to over US$4 billion in 2024, making it the third-largest destination for outbound Chinese capital.
Contrary to expectation, some Chinese manufacturers have sped up their Mexico or Brazil investments in the aftermath of the Venezuela incident, according to Jack Lee, founder of the US-based consulting firm Grand Central Advisory.
“[They] want to invest more aggressively into the market because they think that if something happens again, the window of opportunity will be closed,” said Lee, whose firm assists Chinese companies with site selection in Latin America. “Players like them, they don’t care about the geopolitics.”
In Lee’s view, most of the more enthusiastic firms have secured sufficient orders for their new ventures, largely from American customers. For those who have not, he added, the sentiment is not so optimistic; they would prefer to pause their projects in the region.
“The overall sentiment is that for a lot of the bosses, that’s all hard-earned money, so obviously they’re very cautious about it,” Lee said. “How much market share they have in North and South America – that determines their next move.”
Kent Liu, the Chinese founder of a customised apparel printing business based in the US, is now preparing to replicate his production model in Brazil. He plans to import low-cost blank garments from China and Southeast Asia, print them locally and ship finished products directly to clients.
“As long as a project has a clear competitive edge, geopolitical risks don’t change the fundamentals that much,” Liu said. “Risks exist in every overseas destination, but only a handful of countries carry elevated geopolitical exposure.”
He noted that he is closely following the geopolitical climate in Latin America, while still eager to expand in Brazil. “Chinese companies will only localise faster.”


Other investment destinations in the region, such as Argentina and Peru, still operate within functioning market frameworks that Chinese firms can assess and price into their decisions, making abrupt derailment by a systemic political breakdown less likely.
Argentina’s challenges are largely macroeconomic – inflation, currency controls and policy volatility – with low odds of an outright regime rupture, while Peru has a long track record of accommodating Chinese investment despite protracted political friction.
Anbound’s Zhao said that despite geopolitical risk being pushed to the forefront, with rising sanctions exposure becoming a particularly salient concern, the Venezuela coup “will not fundamentally alter Chinese companies’ operations across Latin America in the short term”.
While the immediate spillover to other Latin American markets appears limited, analysts said the Venezuela episode served as a stress test for businesses in the region, offering clues for how quickly geopolitical pressure could reshape operating conditions and giving Beijing cause to consider protection and contingency plans for its companies.
Under a Venezuelan administration approved by Washington, the Economist Intelligence Unit (EIU) said in a report last week, Chinese presence in fields considered sensitive such as telecoms, electricity grids and strategic ports would probably be diluted.
The report’s authors said the Venezuela affair should also serve as a strong incentive for Beijing to reassess how it protects its overseas interests in Latin America amid broader US pressure, and that its infrastructure-focused Belt and Road Initiative may face “a tough 2026” in the region.
The EIU researchers cited Nicaragua as an early indicator of this chilling effect, noting that a Chinese-funded airport project appeared to be paused “in anticipation of US pressure”, and saying other regional economies could become less receptive to Chinese involvement in infrastructure, energy and mining projects.
This uncertainty could squeeze out Chinese capital and narrow the market space and bargaining power of the country’s institutions, said Ren Peng, partner at the Beijing Yunting law firm, which specialises in international legal services.
He suggested an 18- to 24-month observation window to assess the new government’s grip on power and foreign investment policies, as well as any easing of US sanctions, while prioritising asset-light projects, such as technical service contracts, over capital-intensive commitments.
“Chinese institutions need to move from expansion to defence, shifting their focus from scaling up investments to managing geopolitical and sanctions risk, and building layered safeguards to withstand a more complex and hostile operating environment.”
Additional reporting by Frank Chen, Ji Siqi and Mandy Zuo

data.title: Start your 2026 wellness journey right with Hoka, Nike, Apple and more
data.description:
Those looking to stay on top of their health-related New Year’s resolutions should check out this latest round-up of product releases from the world of wellness. Everything from functional sportswear to a fitness subscription service is here, designed to encourage you to get – and to stay – motivated.
American Vintage

Contemporary French brand American Vintage, which was founded in Marseille in 2005, recently launched its first activewear range for men and women. The retro collection features workout bras, sweatshirts, leggings and other elevated sportswear basics in an array of hues – from mint green and lilac to fiery red. Many of the pieces are embossed with the label’s founding year and an “American Vintage Sports Club” logo.
Apple Fitness+

Sticking to your health and wellness resolutions throughout the New Year just got a lot easier. The award-winning subscription service Apple Fitness+, now available in Hong Kong, helps users accomplish their goals while ensuring they remain motivated. The platform offers 12 kinds of workouts, trainer advice, and the chance to create entire playlists from some of the hottest names in music.
Hoka

Running shoe specialists Hoka and female-founded ski and outdoor gear brand Halfdays have created a stylish yet highly functional pair of hiking sneakers specifically designed for the snow. Named Kaha 2 Frost Moc GTX, the style is available in two dreamy colourways – Amethyst/Glazed Cherry and Oat Milk/Spiced Cocoa – and is fitted with PrimaLoft panels for deep warmth and insulation. Perfect for snowy walks and après-ski style.
Nike

Football fans around the world are gearing up for the Fifa World Cup 2026. The US, Mexico and Canada are co-hosting, with the opening match – between Mexico and South Africa – set for June 11 in Mexico City. Whether you’re planning to go or anticipate watching from afar, check out Nike Football’s new Hollywood Keepers collection, featuring eight national goalkeeper jerseys inspired by the bright and brash kits popular in the 1990s and early 2000s. Each one is emblazoned with an outsized star motif as well as spectacular graphics drawing on national themes and emblems.
Sporty & Rich

Sporty & Rich – the Los Angeles-based activewear and lifestyle brand favoured by Gen Z influencers and millennials – has rolled out its latest collection of preppy athleisure wear at Lane Crawford. We’re fond of these Ivy varsity-style zipped hoodies, which are cut from pre-shrunken garment-dyed cotton and embroidered with the label’s moniker across the chest. Wear it with jeans and classic leather loafers for a smarter take on weekend dressing.
